---
title: "Minimizing in Many Variables: Setting the Gradient to Zero"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CURVE   = "#2b3a67"    # the objective / surfaces
TANGENT = "#c1121f"    # the optimum / gradients
ACCENT  = "#0a7d4d"    # highlights / stationary points
GREY    = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the third article of the Optimization
> track and the hinge of its on-ramp. It assumes [OPTM-00 *What Is an Optimization
> Problem?*] for the vocabulary — **decision variable**, **objective**, **feasible
> set**, **local** vs. **global** minimum — and [OPTM-01 *One-Dimensional
> Minimization*] for the template we are about to generalize: at an interior minimum
> the derivative is zero ($f'(x)=0$), and the second derivative sorts the flat spots.
> Here the single variable becomes a *vector*, the derivative becomes the **gradient**
> $\nabla f$, and the second-derivative test becomes the **Hessian test** — with a new
> failure mode, the **saddle point**, that has no one-dimensional shadow.
>
> **Heavy cross-track dependence.** This article *uses* but does **not** re-derive the
> multivariable-calculus objects it runs on — the **gradient** $\nabla f$,
> **directional derivatives**, the **Hessian** $H$, **quadratic forms** $x^\top A x$,
> and **positive-/negative-(semi)definiteness**. These belong to the *Geometry of
> Data / Applied Linear Algebra* track and are flagged at the exact point each is
> first needed. We assume you can *compute* a gradient and a Hessian; we teach what to
> *do* with them. It sets up the **convexity spine** ([OPTM-03]–[OPTM-05], which says
> *when* a stationary point is the global minimum) and the **descent spine**
> ([OPTM-06] onward, which says *how* to reach one without a closed form).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

We are handed a function $f$ of several variables — a vector input $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n$
— and asked for the input that makes it smallest. This article generalizes [OPTM-01]'s
"set the derivative to zero" to many dimensions, with every step on the page. The
**first-order condition** becomes **stationarity**, $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)=\mathbf{0}$:
the gradient is the direction of steepest *ascent*, so at a minimum it must vanish, or
else there is still a downhill direction to walk. We derive this from the
directional-derivative picture rather than assert it. The **second-order test** becomes
the **Hessian test**: the matrix of second partials must be **positive-definite** for a
local minimum, **negative-definite** for a maximum, and when it is **indefinite** we meet
the genuinely new object — the **saddle point**, a stationary point that is a minimum
along one direction and a maximum along another. We picture all three on $x^2+y^2$,
$-x^2-y^2$, and $x^2-y^2$, explain why saddles dominate high-dimensional machine-learning
landscapes (a forward look to [OPTM-11]), and carry the machinery through to its headline
payoff: deriving the **OLS normal equations** $X^\top X\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}=X^\top
\mathbf{y}$ as nothing more than $\nabla\|\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta\|^2=\mathbf{0}$ —
the exact move the Regression track links here for.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
At an interior minimum of a smooth function of many variables, **the gradient is the
zero vector**: $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)=\mathbf{0}$. This is the multivariate
"flat tangent." It is *necessary* — the gradient points in the direction of steepest
ascent, so $-\nabla f$ points steepest downhill, and a nonzero gradient is a guaranteed
direction of decrease. It is **not sufficient**: the same condition marks maxima and
**saddle points**. The **Hessian** $H$ — the matrix of second partial derivatives —
disambiguates, exactly as $f''$ did in one dimension: $H$ **positive-definite** (curving
up in *every* direction) confirms a minimum, **negative-definite** a maximum, and
**indefinite** (up in some directions, down in others) a **saddle**. Know all three
clauses and you know precisely when "set the gradient to zero" is the whole story and
when it is a trap — and the saddle is a trap with no one-dimensional warning.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The equation $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ will look like a
list of "set each partial derivative to zero" rules, but by the end you should see it as
*forced*: it is the precise statement that **no direction is downhill**. The new clause
— the saddle — is not a footnote. In the high-dimensional landscapes of modern machine
learning, the overwhelming majority of stationary points are saddles, and recognizing
them is why the descent algorithms in the rest of the track are built the way they are.

# Why we are here: the problem that forces the question {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **what does it mean for a function of
many variables to be "at the bottom," and how does calculus detect it?** Every step
below answers a piece of that.

## The setting, inherited from OPTM-01 {#sec-setting}

[OPTM-01] solved the one-variable problem: find $x^\star=\arg\min_x f(x)$ by setting
$f'(x)=0$ and checking $f''$. But almost nothing we actually want to optimize has a
single knob. A regression line has an intercept *and* a slope — two variables, the case
[REGR-01] worked. A regression with $p$ predictors has $p+1$ coefficients. A neural
network has millions of parameters. The objective is a single number (a cost, a negative
log-likelihood, a loss), but it depends on a whole *vector* of inputs:

$$
f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}, \qquad
\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^\top \;\mapsto\; f(\mathbf{x}),
$$ {#eq-fn}

and we want

$$
\mathbf{x}^\star = \operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\mathbf{x}\in C} f(\mathbf{x}),
$$ {#eq-argmin}

the *location* of the smallest output over the feasible set $C$ (here usually all of
$\mathbb{R}^n$). The question of whether a minimizer *exists* was OPTM-00's job; the
question of *when a stationary point is the global one* belongs to the convexity spine
([OPTM-03]–[OPTM-04]). This article answers the question in between: **how does calculus
locate a candidate minimizer when the input is a vector, and how do we test it?**

This matters because it is the literal engine of model fitting. Every method in the rest
of the series that has a *closed form* — ordinary least squares, ridge regression,
linear discriminant analysis, the Gaussian MLE — gets that closed form by exactly the
move of this article: write the objective, take the gradient, set it to zero, solve the
resulting equations. And every method *without* a closed form (logistic regression,
neural nets) is fit by an algorithm whose entire job is to drive $\nabla f$ toward
$\mathbf{0}$. So $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ is not one technique among many. It is the
target that the whole field is organized around hitting.

## What is genuinely new in many dimensions {#sec-whats-new}

You might reasonably ask: *if this is "just" the 1-D test done $n$ times, why a whole
article?* Three things are genuinely new, and each is a place practitioners get burned.

1. **Direction matters now.** In one dimension there are only two ways to move — left and
   right. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ there are infinitely many directions, and "downhill" must
   hold in *all* of them at once. This is what forces the gradient, a single object that
   packages the slope in every direction.
2. **A new kind of stationary point: the saddle.** With two or more variables a function
   can curve *up* along one axis and *down* along another at the same flat spot — a
   mountain pass. There is no such thing in one dimension. Saddles satisfy
   $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ but are neither minima nor maxima, and in high dimensions they
   vastly outnumber both.
3. **The second-derivative test becomes a matrix question.** "Is the curvature positive?"
   becomes "is the *Hessian matrix* positive-definite?" — a linear-algebra property,
   answered by eigenvalues, that quietly imports the entire machinery of quadratic forms.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why can't I just minimize one variable at a time — fix all but $x_1$, minimize over
$x_1$, then move to $x_2$, and so on?"* You *can*, and it is a real algorithm —
**coordinate descent**, owned by [OPTM-10] — but it is not the same as $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$,
and it can crawl or stall on objectives with coupling between the variables (the contours
are tilted ellipses, not axis-aligned). The condition $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ asks for *all*
partial derivatives to vanish *simultaneously*, at one point; coordinate descent reaches
that point only in the limit, one axis at a time. The interviewer is checking whether you
see that "flat in every direction at once" (the gradient condition) is a stronger,
cleaner statement than "I optimized each coordinate in turn."
:::

# Intuition before symbols: the landscape {#sec-intuition}

Picture the graph of $f$ as a *landscape* over the plane (take $n=2$ so we can see it):
above each input point $(x_1, x_2)$ the surface has height $f(x_1, x_2)$. Minimizing $f$
is finding the lowest point of this terrain. Standing somewhere on it, you can now walk in
**any** compass direction, not just east–west.

The 1-D logic carries over almost verbatim, with one upgrade. In one dimension, *a nonzero
slope was a downhill arrow*. Here, **the gradient is that arrow** — a single vector that
points in the steepest *uphill* direction, whose length is how steep. If the gradient is
nonzero, then walking *against* it (the direction $-\nabla f$) takes you strictly downhill,
so you were not at the bottom. The only way no direction is downhill is if the arrow has
**zero length**: $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$. That is the whole first-order story, stated before
any algebra. @fig-landscape draws the terrain and the downhill arrows.

```{python}
#| label: fig-landscape
#| fig-cap: "The optimization landscape for $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$. **Left:** the surface, a single bowl, with its minimum at the origin (green). **Right:** the contour map with the negative-gradient field $-\\nabla f$ drawn as arrows — at every point the arrow points straight downhill toward the minimum, and its length is the steepness. Where the arrows shrink to nothing (the center) is exactly where $\\nabla f=\\mathbf{0}$: the bottom of the bowl. A nonzero gradient is always a downhill arrow."
f = lambda X, Y: X**2 + Y**2
xs = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 60)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(xs, xs)
Z = f(X, Y)

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(9.0, 3.9))
axL = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1, projection="3d")
axL.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.85, linewidth=0, antialiased=True)
axL.scatter([0], [0], [0], color=ACCENT, s=60, depthshade=False)
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$"); axL.set_zlabel("$f$")
axL.set_title("$f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$: one bowl")
axL.view_init(elev=30, azim=-120)

axR = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2)
xs2 = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 18)
Xq, Yq = np.meshgrid(xs2, xs2)
# negative gradient of x^2+y^2 is (-2x, -2y)
U, V = -2*Xq, -2*Yq
axR.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=12, colors=GREY, linewidths=0.7, alpha=0.7)
axR.quiver(Xq, Yq, U, V, color=TANGENT, alpha=0.8, width=0.005, scale=60)
axR.scatter([0], [0], color=ACCENT, s=80, zorder=5)
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("$y$")
axR.set_title(r"$-\nabla f$ points downhill")
axR.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Three warnings are already visible and we will pay all three off. First, *flat is not the
same as lowest*: the top of a hill ($-x^2-y^2$) is flat too. Second — and new — a flat
spot can be a **saddle**, downhill in some directions and uphill in others, like a
mountain pass; the gradient vanishes there too. Third, the argument that "$-\nabla f$ is
downhill" needs every direction to be available — at the boundary of a constrained set one
direction is forbidden, and a gradient can persist at the true minimum (the multivariate
echo of OPTM-01's boundary case, taken up by Lagrange multipliers in [OPTM-13]).

# The objects we are importing {#sec-objects}

This article *uses* the gradient and the Hessian; the Geometry of Data track *builds*
them. To keep the derivations self-contained at the level we need — without re-deriving
multivariable calculus from scratch — we restate the three imported objects precisely,
the way [OPTM-01] restated the definition of the derivative. If these are rusty, this
section is your refresher; if they are fresh, skim it for our notation.

## The partial derivative {#sec-partial}

The **partial derivative** of $f(\mathbf{x})$ with respect to $x_i$ is computed by the
rule from [REGR-01] and [OPTM-01]: **treat every other variable as a constant, then
differentiate normally with the Calc-1 rules.** The symbol is $\partial$, not $d$, purely
to signal "other variables are being held still." Formally it is the one-dimensional
derivative taken along the $i$-th axis:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}(\mathbf{x})
= \lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(\mathbf{x}+h\,\mathbf{e}_i)-f(\mathbf{x})}{h},
$$ {#eq-partial}

where $\mathbf{e}_i$ is the unit vector with a $1$ in slot $i$ and zeros elsewhere — so
$\mathbf{x}+h\,\mathbf{e}_i$ nudges only the $i$-th coordinate. Read it as a rate: *how
fast $f$ changes as you push the $i$-th input, holding the rest fixed.*

## The gradient {#sec-gradient}

::: {#def-gradient}
**Definition (gradient).** The **gradient** of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}$ is the vector that
stacks all $n$ partial derivatives:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x})
= \begin{pmatrix}
\partial f/\partial x_1 \\ \partial f/\partial x_2 \\ \vdots \\ \partial f/\partial x_n
\end{pmatrix}.
$$
:::

The gradient is the multivariate replacement for the single number $f'(x)$. It is itself a
*vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$* — one slope per input direction, bundled together. Two facts
about it are the engine of this whole article, and we will **derive both** in @sec-first-order
rather than assert them: (i) it points in the direction of steepest *ascent*, and (ii) it
is the gradient's vanishing, $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$, that signals a flat spot.

## The Hessian {#sec-hessian-obj}

::: {#def-hessian}
**Definition (Hessian).** The **Hessian** of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}$ is the $n\times n$ matrix
of all second partial derivatives:

$$
H(\mathbf{x}) = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x})
= \begin{pmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1\partial x_n} \\[8pt]
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2\partial x_n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[2pt]
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2}
\end{pmatrix}.
$$
:::

The $(i,j)$ entry is $\partial^2 f/\partial x_i \partial x_j$ — differentiate first by
$x_j$, then by $x_i$. The Hessian is the multivariate replacement for the single number
$f''(x)$. For the smooth functions we optimize, the mixed partials are equal,
$\partial^2 f/\partial x_i\partial x_j = \partial^2 f/\partial x_j\partial x_i$ (this is
**Clairaut's / Schwarz's theorem**, a standard result of the Geometry track), so $H$ is a
**symmetric** matrix — a fact we will lean on, because symmetric matrices have real
eigenvalues and that is what makes the Hessian test work.

::: {.callout-note title="What we borrowed, and where it is built"}
This article imports four objects from the **Geometry of Data / Applied Linear Algebra**
track and does not re-derive them: the **gradient** $\nabla f$ (@def-gradient), the
**Hessian** $H$ (@def-hessian), **quadratic forms** $\mathbf{v}^\top A\mathbf{v}$
(@sec-second-order), and **positive-/negative-(semi)definiteness** with their
eigenvalue characterization (@sec-classify). We restated each at the level we use it so
the logic runs end to end. The complete development — why the gradient is perpendicular to
level sets, the spectral theorem for symmetric matrices, the geometry of quadratic forms —
lives in that track. Everything *we do with* these objects is derived in full here.
:::

# The first-order condition, derived {#sec-first-order}

We now turn the downhill-arrow picture into mathematics, deriving $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$
from the directional-derivative idea — nothing assumed above what @sec-objects restated.

## The directional derivative: slope along any direction {#sec-directional}

A partial derivative measures the slope along an *axis*. But at a minimum we need *no*
direction to be downhill, not just the axis directions. So we need the slope along an
**arbitrary** direction. Fix a unit vector $\mathbf{u}$ (length $\|\mathbf{u}\|=1$,
pointing wherever you like). The **directional derivative** of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}$ in the
direction $\mathbf{u}$ is the rate of change of $f$ as you step along $\mathbf{u}$:

$$
D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x})
= \lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(\mathbf{x}+h\,\mathbf{u})-f(\mathbf{x})}{h}.
$$ {#eq-dirderiv}

This is literally a *one-dimensional* derivative: define the single-variable function
$g(h)=f(\mathbf{x}+h\,\mathbf{u})$, which slices the landscape along the line through
$\mathbf{x}$ in direction $\mathbf{u}$, and then $D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x})=g'(0)$. So
everything [OPTM-01] proved about $g'$ applies slice by slice.

Now the key identity, which connects the directional derivative to the gradient. Apply the
**multivariable chain rule** (a Geometry-track result we use, not rebuild) to
$g(h)=f(x_1+hu_1,\dots,x_n+hu_n)$. Each input coordinate $x_i+hu_i$ has derivative $u_i$
with respect to $h$, so the chain rule sums one term per coordinate:

$$
g'(h) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}(\mathbf{x}+h\,\mathbf{u})\cdot u_i .
$$

Evaluate at $h=0$ and recognize the right-hand side as a dot product of the gradient
vector with the direction vector:

$$
D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}) = g'(0)
= \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}(\mathbf{x})\, u_i
= \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^\top \mathbf{u}.
$$ {#eq-dir-as-dot}

Read @eq-dir-as-dot slowly: **the slope of the landscape in direction $\mathbf{u}$ is the
dot product of the gradient with $\mathbf{u}$.** The gradient alone, dotted against any
direction you choose, hands you the slope that way. This single formula carries the entire
first-order theory.

## Why the gradient is the steepest-ascent direction {#sec-steepest}

The dot product has a geometric form we now use (the **law of cosines / dot-product angle
formula**, from the Geometry track): for vectors $\mathbf{a},\mathbf{b}$ with angle
$\theta$ between them, $\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}=\|\mathbf{a}\|\,\|\mathbf{b}\|\cos\theta$.
Apply it to @eq-dir-as-dot with $\mathbf{u}$ a *unit* vector ($\|\mathbf{u}\|=1$):

$$
D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x})
= \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^\top\mathbf{u}
= \|\nabla f(\mathbf{x})\|\,\underbrace{\|\mathbf{u}\|}_{=1}\cos\theta
= \|\nabla f(\mathbf{x})\|\cos\theta,
$$ {#eq-steepest}

where $\theta$ is the angle between the gradient and the direction $\mathbf{u}$ you walk.
The only thing that varies as you spin $\mathbf{u}$ around is $\cos\theta$, which ranges
over $[-1,1]$. So the slope $D_{\mathbf{u}}f$ is:

- **largest** ($=+\|\nabla f\|$) when $\cos\theta=1$, i.e. $\theta=0$: walking *along* the
  gradient. The gradient is the **steepest-ascent** direction.
- **most negative** ($=-\|\nabla f\|$) when $\cos\theta=-1$, i.e. $\theta=180^\circ$:
  walking *against* the gradient. So $-\nabla f$ is the **steepest-descent** direction —
  the fact the entire descent spine ([OPTM-06] onward) is built on.
- **zero** when $\cos\theta=0$, i.e. $\theta=90^\circ$: walking *perpendicular* to the
  gradient keeps $f$ momentarily unchanged. That is why the gradient is perpendicular to
  the level sets (contour lines).

We have now *derived* the two facts promised in @sec-gradient: the gradient points
steepest uphill, and $-\nabla f$ steepest downhill. @fig-steepest visualizes that the dot
product is maximized along the gradient.

```{python}
#| label: fig-steepest
#| fig-cap: "Why $-\\nabla f$ is the downhill direction. At a point on the landscape of $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$, the directional slope $D_{\\mathbf{u}}f=\\|\\nabla f\\|\\cos\\theta$ traces a cosine as the walking direction $\\mathbf{u}$ rotates through $360^\\circ$ (left). It is largest along the gradient ($\\theta=0$), most negative against it ($\\theta=180^\\circ$), and exactly zero perpendicular to it ($\\theta=90^\\circ$). Right: the same point with the gradient (red) and its perpendicular contour line — the contour is precisely the zero-slope direction."
pt = np.array([1.2, 0.8])
grad = 2*pt                       # gradient of x^2+y^2 is (2x, 2y)
gnorm = np.linalg.norm(grad)
thetas = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 400)
slopes = gnorm * np.cos(thetas)   # D_u f along direction at angle theta from grad

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.7))
axL.plot(np.degrees(thetas), slopes, color=CURVE)
axL.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
axL.scatter([0], [gnorm], color=TANGENT, s=55, zorder=5)
axL.scatter([180], [-gnorm], color=ACCENT, s=55, zorder=5)
axL.annotate("steepest ASCENT\n($\\nabla f$)", (0, gnorm), (40, gnorm*0.55),
             fontsize=8.5, color=TANGENT)
axL.annotate("steepest DESCENT\n($-\\nabla f$)", (180, -gnorm), (95, -gnorm*0.55),
             fontsize=8.5, color=ACCENT)
axL.set_xlabel(r"angle $\theta$ from gradient (deg)")
axL.set_ylabel(r"directional slope $D_{\mathbf{u}}f$")
axL.set_title(r"$D_{\mathbf{u}}f=\|\nabla f\|\cos\theta$")

xs = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 60)
Xg, Yg = np.meshgrid(xs, xs)
axR.contour(Xg, Yg, Xg**2 + Yg**2, levels=12, colors=GREY, linewidths=0.7, alpha=0.7)
axR.quiver([pt[0]], [pt[1]], [grad[0]], [grad[1]], color=TANGENT,
           angles="xy", scale_units="xy", scale=3, width=0.012)
# perpendicular (tangent to contour) direction
perp = np.array([-grad[1], grad[0]]) / gnorm
axR.plot([pt[0]-0.8*perp[0], pt[0]+0.8*perp[0]],
         [pt[1]-0.8*perp[1], pt[1]+0.8*perp[1]], color=ACCENT, lw=2.0)
axR.scatter([pt[0]], [pt[1]], color=CURVE, s=50, zorder=5)
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("$y$")
axR.set_title(r"$\nabla f \perp$ contour")
axR.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The stationarity condition {#sec-stationarity}

Now we prove the headline. We use the same contradiction structure as [OPTM-01]'s
@thm-fermat, upgraded from "left/right step" to "step along any direction."

::: {#thm-stationarity}
**Theorem (first-order necessary condition).** Let $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ be
differentiable at a point $\mathbf{x}^\star$ that is **interior** to the feasible set $C$
(some open ball around $\mathbf{x}^\star$ lies inside $C$). If $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is a
**local minimum** of $f$ on $C$, then

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = \mathbf{0}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We argue by contradiction. Suppose $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)\neq\mathbf{0}$; we exhibit a
nearby feasible point with a strictly smaller value, contradicting "local minimum."

Because the gradient is nonzero, we may choose the **steepest-descent direction**

$$
\mathbf{u} = -\frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)}{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)\|},
$$

a unit vector (we divided by its own length). By @eq-dir-as-dot the slope along
$\mathbf{u}$ is

$$
D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}^\star)
= \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top\mathbf{u}
= \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top\!\left(-\frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)}{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)\|}\right)
= -\frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)}{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)\|}
= -\frac{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)\|^2}{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)\|}
= -\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)\| < 0,
$$

where we used the defining fact that
$\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}=\|\mathbf{a}\|^2$ (a vector dotted with itself is its squared
length). The slope along $\mathbf{u}$ is *strictly negative*.

Now restrict $f$ to the line through $\mathbf{x}^\star$ in direction $\mathbf{u}$: let
$g(h)=f(\mathbf{x}^\star+h\,\mathbf{u})$, a single-variable function with $g'(0)=
D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}^\star)<0$. This is exactly the one-dimensional situation of
[OPTM-01]'s @thm-fermat: a negative derivative at $h=0$ means, by the definition of the
derivative @eq-dirderiv, that the difference quotient
$\bigl(g(h)-g(0)\bigr)/h$ is negative for all small enough $h>0$, and with a positive
denominator the numerator must be negative — so $g(h)<g(0)$ for some small $h>0$. Spelling
that out:

$$
g(h) = f(\mathbf{x}^\star + h\,\mathbf{u}) < f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = g(0).
$$

Because $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is **interior** to $C$, for small enough $h$ the point
$\mathbf{x}^\star+h\,\mathbf{u}$ still lies inside $C$ and is allowed. We have produced a
feasible neighbor with a strictly smaller value, so $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is **not** a local
minimum — contradiction. The only escape is $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)=\mathbf{0}$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

That proof is the rigorous version of the downhill-arrow picture: **a nonzero gradient is
a guaranteed direction of decrease (namely $-\nabla f$), and a guaranteed downhill
direction is incompatible with being at the bottom.** Notice the proof never used that
$\mathbf{x}^\star$ was a *minimum* specifically — the same argument at a *maximum* walks
*uphill* along $+\nabla f$ to find a higher point. So $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ catches **all**
interior extrema and, as we will see, saddles too. That is precisely why it cannot, by
itself, tell them apart.

::: {.callout-important title="The word that does the work: *stationary*"}
A point where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})=\mathbf{0}$ is called a **stationary point** (or
**critical point**), exactly as in one dimension. @thm-stationarity says **every interior
local extremum is a stationary point** — it casts the net. It does **not** say the reverse:
a stationary point may be a minimum, a maximum, or a *saddle*. Sorting the catch is the job
of the Hessian test (@sec-second-order). This necessary-but-not-sufficient pattern is the
backbone of the whole Optimization track — $f'=0$ in [OPTM-01], $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ here,
the KKT conditions in [OPTM-14] — every one is a "stationarity net" followed by a
second-order or convexity check.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"$\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ is one vector equation. How many scalar equations is that, and what
do you do with them?"* It is **$n$ scalar equations** — one per component, $\partial
f/\partial x_i=0$ for $i=1,\dots,n$ — in the $n$ unknowns $x_1,\dots,x_n$. So setting the
gradient to zero gives you a square system to solve. When $f$ is a quadratic (the
least-squares case), each $\partial f/\partial x_i$ is *linear* in the unknowns, so
$\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ is a **linear system** $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ you solve by linear
algebra — this is literally how the normal equations arise in @sec-ols. When $f$ is not
quadratic (logistic loss, say), the system is nonlinear, usually has no closed form, and
you solve it iteratively — gradient descent ([OPTM-06]) or Newton's method ([OPTM-09]).
The interviewer wants to hear "$n$ equations, $n$ unknowns; linear if $f$ is quadratic,
else iterate."
:::

# Sorting the catch: the Hessian test {#sec-second-order}

Solving $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ hands us a list of stationary points. Each is a minimum, a
maximum, or a saddle. We need a *local* test to classify each one — the multivariate
analogue of "$f''>0$ means minimum."

## What curvature means with a vector input {#sec-curvature}

In one dimension, $f''(x^\star)>0$ said the curve bends upward — the slope is increasing as
you pass through, tracing a valley. In many dimensions there is a *different curvature in
every direction*, and a minimum requires the surface to bend **upward in all of them at
once**. The Hessian is exactly the object that packages all those curvatures, and the tool
that reads "upward in every direction" off the Hessian is the **second-order Taylor
expansion**.

Near a stationary point $\mathbf{x}^\star$ (where $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$), the second-order
Taylor expansion of $f$ — a Geometry-track result we use here, the multivariate
generalization of the Calc-1 expansion $f(x^\star+h)\approx f(x^\star)+\tfrac12
f''(x^\star)h^2$ — is

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^\star + \mathbf{v})
\;\approx\; f(\mathbf{x}^\star)
\;+\; \underbrace{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^\top \mathbf{v}}_{=\,0\ \text{(stationary)}}
\;+\; \tfrac12\,\mathbf{v}^\top H(\mathbf{x}^\star)\,\mathbf{v}
\;=\; f(\mathbf{x}^\star) + \tfrac12\,\mathbf{v}^\top H \mathbf{v},
$$ {#eq-taylor2}

where $\mathbf{v}$ is a small displacement away from $\mathbf{x}^\star$. The linear term
*vanishes* because we are at a stationary point — that is the whole reason the Hessian, not
the gradient, governs what happens next. So whether $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is a minimum hinges
entirely on the sign of the **quadratic form**

$$
Q(\mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{v}^\top H \mathbf{v}.
$$ {#eq-qform}

If $Q(\mathbf{v})>0$ for *every* nonzero displacement $\mathbf{v}$, then by @eq-taylor2 the
value $f(\mathbf{x}^\star+\mathbf{v})>f(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ in *every* direction — the
definition of a strict local minimum. If $Q(\mathbf{v})<0$ in every direction, it is a
maximum. If $Q$ is positive in some directions and negative in others, the point goes up
some ways and down others: a **saddle**. The names for these three cases are exactly the
**definiteness** of the matrix $H$.

## Definiteness: the multivariate sign of $f''$ {#sec-classify}

::: {#def-definiteness}
**Definition (definiteness of a symmetric matrix).** Let $H$ be a symmetric $n\times n$
matrix and $Q(\mathbf{v})=\mathbf{v}^\top H\mathbf{v}$ its quadratic form. Then $H$ is:

- **positive-definite** if $Q(\mathbf{v})>0$ for every $\mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf{0}$;
- **negative-definite** if $Q(\mathbf{v})<0$ for every $\mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf{0}$;
- **indefinite** if $Q(\mathbf{v})>0$ for some $\mathbf{v}$ and $Q(\mathbf{v})<0$ for
  another;
- **positive-semidefinite** if $Q(\mathbf{v})\ge 0$ for all $\mathbf{v}$ (allowing
  equality), and **negative-semidefinite** symmetrically.
:::

These are *the* objects the Geometry track owns; we import them and the single fact that
makes them checkable in practice. A symmetric matrix has **real eigenvalues**
$\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n$ (the **spectral theorem**), and the sign of the quadratic form
is read directly off them:

$$
H \text{ positive-definite} \iff \text{all } \lambda_i>0,
\qquad
H \text{ negative-definite} \iff \text{all } \lambda_i<0,
\qquad
H \text{ indefinite} \iff \text{eigenvalues of both signs.}
$$ {#eq-eig}

Why this is true is the Geometry track's job, but the one-line intuition is worth carrying:
the eigenvectors are the axes along which the quadratic form is "pure," and along the
eigenvector for $\lambda_i$ the form equals $\lambda_i\|\mathbf{v}\|^2$ — so the eigenvalue
*is* the curvature along that axis. All curvatures positive $\Rightarrow$ a bowl; mixed
signs $\Rightarrow$ a saddle. We will use @eq-eig as our computational test throughout.

::: {.callout-note title="A cheaper test for $2\times 2$ Hessians"}
For the $2\times2$ case — which covers every two-parameter model and all our hand examples
— you need not find eigenvalues. With $H=\begin{psmallmatrix} a & b \\ b & c\end{psmallmatrix}$,
the **determinant** is $\det H = ac-b^2$ and the **trace** is $a+c$. Then: $\det H>0$ and
$a>0$ $\Rightarrow$ positive-definite (minimum); $\det H>0$ and $a<0$ $\Rightarrow$
negative-definite (maximum); $\det H<0$ $\Rightarrow$ indefinite (saddle). This is precisely
the two-variable second-derivative test from multivariable calculus, and it is the *same*
determinant condition $S_{xx}>0$ that [REGR-01] used to confirm the OLS bowl. (It works
because for a $2\times2$ symmetric matrix $\det H=\lambda_1\lambda_2$ and
$\operatorname{tr}H=\lambda_1+\lambda_2$, so the sign pattern of the eigenvalues is pinned
down by the determinant and one diagonal entry.)
:::

## The test, stated {#sec-hessian-test}

::: {#thm-hessian}
**Theorem (Hessian / second-order test).** Let $f$ be twice continuously differentiable
near a stationary point $\mathbf{x}^\star$ (so $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)=\mathbf{0}$),
with Hessian $H=H(\mathbf{x}^\star)$. Then:

1. if $H$ is **positive-definite**, $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is a **strict local minimum**;
2. if $H$ is **negative-definite**, $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is a **strict local maximum**;
3. if $H$ is **indefinite**, $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is a **saddle point** — neither;
4. if $H$ is only **semidefinite** (some eigenvalue is exactly $0$), the test is
   **inconclusive** — the multivariate echo of the $f''=0$ case in [OPTM-01].
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove (1); (2) is the same argument applied to $-f$ (whose Hessian is $-H$, flipping all
signs), (3) we establish by direct construction below, and (4) we settle with explicit
counterexamples in @sec-semidefinite.

Assume $H$ is positive-definite. Take any nonzero displacement $\mathbf{v}$. By the
second-order Taylor expansion @eq-taylor2 with the linear term gone,

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^\star + t\mathbf{v}) - f(\mathbf{x}^\star)
= \tfrac12 (t\mathbf{v})^\top H (t\mathbf{v}) + r(t)
= \tfrac12 t^2\,\mathbf{v}^\top H\mathbf{v} + r(t),
$$

where we scaled the step by $t>0$ and pulled the scalars out of the quadratic form
($(t\mathbf{v})^\top H(t\mathbf{v}) = t^2\,\mathbf{v}^\top H\mathbf{v}$), and $r(t)$ is the
Taylor remainder, which shrinks faster than $t^2$ (formally $r(t)/t^2\to 0$ as $t\to 0$, the
defining property of the second-order remainder). Because $H$ is positive-definite,
$\mathbf{v}^\top H\mathbf{v}>0$ — call it $c>0$. Then

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^\star + t\mathbf{v}) - f(\mathbf{x}^\star)
= \tfrac12 c\,t^2 + r(t)
= t^2\left(\tfrac12 c + \frac{r(t)}{t^2}\right).
$$

As $t\to 0$ the bracket tends to $\tfrac12 c>0$, so for all sufficiently small $t>0$ the
bracket is positive, and $t^2>0$, making the whole right side positive:
$f(\mathbf{x}^\star+t\mathbf{v})>f(\mathbf{x}^\star)$. This holds for *every* direction
$\mathbf{v}$, so $f$ strictly increases in every direction away from $\mathbf{x}^\star$ —
exactly a strict local minimum.

For **(3)**, indefiniteness gives a direction $\mathbf{v}_+$ with
$\mathbf{v}_+^\top H\mathbf{v}_+>0$ and a direction $\mathbf{v}_-$ with
$\mathbf{v}_-^\top H\mathbf{v}_-<0$. Repeating the argument above along $\mathbf{v}_+$ shows
$f$ *increases* that way, and along $\mathbf{v}_-$ shows $f$ *decreases* — so
$\mathbf{x}^\star$ is below some neighbors and above others. It is neither a minimum nor a
maximum: a saddle. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The proof says in slow motion what the curvature picture shows: positive-definite means the
bowl opens upward in every direction, so you sit at the bottom; indefinite means at least
one direction tips downward, and a single downhill direction is enough to disqualify a
minimum — even though the gradient is zero.

# The three faces of a stationary point {#sec-three-faces}

The cleanest way to internalize @thm-hessian is to watch all three cases on the simplest
functions that exhibit them — the multivariate analogue of [OPTM-01]'s $x^2$, $-x^2$, $x^3$
trio. Each is a pure quadratic, so its Hessian is *constant* and the classification is
exact, not just local.

## The bowl: $f=x^2+y^2$ (positive-definite, a minimum) {#sec-bowl}

Take $f(x,y)=x^2+y^2$. The gradient and Hessian:

$$
\nabla f = \begin{pmatrix} 2x \\ 2y \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
H = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}.
$$

Stationary point: $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ forces $2x=0$ and $2y=0$, so $(0,0)$ is the only
one. The Hessian is diagonal with entries $2,2$ — both eigenvalues are $2>0$, so $H$ is
**positive-definite**, and $(0,0)$ is a strict local minimum (in fact global: the form
$x^2+y^2\ge0$ with equality only at the origin). The quadratic form
$Q(\mathbf{v})=2v_1^2+2v_2^2>0$ for every nonzero $\mathbf{v}$ — up in *every* direction.

## The dome: $f=-x^2-y^2$ (negative-definite, a maximum) {#sec-dome}

Flip the signs: $f(x,y)=-x^2-y^2$. Now
$\nabla f=(-2x,-2y)^\top$, again zero only at $(0,0)$, and
$H=\begin{psmallmatrix}-2&0\\0&-2\end{psmallmatrix}$, eigenvalues $-2,-2$, both negative —
**negative-definite**. The origin is a strict local (and global) maximum: $Q(\mathbf{v})=
-2v_1^2-2v_2^2<0$ in every direction, down everywhere.

## The saddle: $f=x^2-y^2$ (indefinite, neither) {#sec-saddle}

Now the genuinely new case, impossible in one dimension. Take $f(x,y)=x^2-y^2$:

$$
\nabla f = \begin{pmatrix} 2x \\ -2y \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
H = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}.
$$

The gradient vanishes only at $(0,0)$ — it *is* a stationary point, $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$
holds. But the Hessian has eigenvalues $2$ and $-2$, **opposite signs**: $H$ is
**indefinite**. The quadratic form $Q(\mathbf{v})=2v_1^2-2v_2^2$ is *positive* along the
$x$-axis ($\mathbf{v}=(1,0)$: $Q=2$) and *negative* along the $y$-axis
($\mathbf{v}=(0,1)$: $Q=-2$). Walk east–west from the origin and you climb; walk
north–south and you descend. The origin is a **mountain pass** — a saddle. A 1-D search
along the $x$-axis would swear it found a minimum; a search along the $y$-axis would swear
it found a maximum. Both are fooled, because the truth requires looking in *every*
direction at once. @fig-three-faces draws all three.

```{python}
#| label: fig-three-faces
#| fig-cap: "The three faces of a stationary point, each with $\\nabla f=\\mathbf{0}$ at the origin. **Left:** $x^2+y^2$ — Hessian positive-definite (eigenvalues $2,2$), a bowl, the origin is a minimum. **Middle:** $-x^2-y^2$ — negative-definite (eigenvalues $-2,-2$), a dome, a maximum. **Right:** $x^2-y^2$ — indefinite (eigenvalues $2,-2$), a saddle: up along $x$, down along $y$. The gradient is zero at all three centers; only the Hessian tells them apart, and only the saddle has no one-dimensional analogue."
xs = np.linspace(-1.6, 1.6, 60)
Xg, Yg = np.meshgrid(xs, xs)
surfaces = [
    (Xg**2 + Yg**2,   "$x^2+y^2$:  min",     "pos-def $(2,2)$"),
    (-Xg**2 - Yg**2,  "$-x^2-y^2$:  max",    "neg-def $(-2,-2)$"),
    (Xg**2 - Yg**2,   "$x^2-y^2$:  saddle",  "indef $(2,-2)$"),
]
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(9.4, 3.4))
for k, (Z, ttl, sub) in enumerate(surfaces):
    ax = fig.add_subplot(1, 3, k+1, projection="3d")
    ax.plot_surface(Xg, Yg, Z, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.9, linewidth=0, antialiased=True)
    ax.scatter([0], [0], [0], color=TANGENT, s=45, depthshade=False)
    ax.set_title(ttl, fontsize=10.5)
    ax.set_xlabel(sub, fontsize=8)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([]); ax.set_zticks([])
    ax.view_init(elev=26, azim=-130)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is a saddle point, and why is it the headache of high-dimensional optimization?"*
A **saddle point** is a stationary point ($\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$) whose Hessian is
**indefinite** — the function curves up along some directions and down along others, like
a horse's saddle or a mountain pass. The reason it dominates high-dimensional problems is a
counting argument worth being able to give: at a stationary point of a "generic" function
in $\mathbb{R}^n$, the Hessian has $n$ eigenvalues, each roughly equally likely positive or
negative. For the point to be a *minimum*, **all $n$ must be positive** — an event that
gets exponentially rare as $n$ grows ($\sim 2^{-n}$ if signs were independent coin flips).
So in a deep network with millions of parameters, almost every stationary point is a saddle,
not a minimum. This is *good* news for training: gradient descent does not get permanently
stuck at a saddle the way it would at a bad local minimum — it can slide off along a
descent direction — but it *slows down* near one, which is exactly what momentum ([OPTM-08])
and stochastic noise ([OPTM-11]) help escape. An interviewer raising this is checking
whether you know that in high dimensions the enemy is saddles, not local minima.
:::

# Worked example 1 — a two-variable cost, by hand {#sec-ex-quadratic}

We start where everything is exact and checkable: a smooth cost in two variables. A
logistics planner sets two dials — a staffing level $x$ and an inventory level $y$ (both in
standardized units) — and the daily operating cost is well modeled by

$$
f(x,y) = x^2 + xy + y^2 - 4x - 5y + 10.
$$ {#eq-ex1}

The cross term $xy$ is the point: staffing and inventory *interact* (more staff makes
inventory cheaper to manage, and vice versa), so this is not two independent 1-D problems —
the variables are coupled, and we must set the whole gradient to zero at once.

**Step 1 — the gradient.** Differentiate $f$ with respect to each variable, treating the
other as constant (@sec-partial). For $\partial f/\partial x$: the $x^2$ gives $2x$, the
$xy$ gives $y$ (treating $y$ as constant, $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(xy)=y$), the $y^2$
gives $0$, the $-4x$ gives $-4$, the $-5y$ and $10$ give $0$:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x + y - 4,
\qquad
\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = x + 2y - 5,
$$

the second by the mirror calculation. So

$$
\nabla f(x,y) = \begin{pmatrix} 2x + y - 4 \\ x + 2y - 5 \end{pmatrix}.
$$

**Step 2 — set it to zero and solve.** Stationarity @thm-stationarity demands both
components vanish, a $2\times2$ **linear system**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x + y &= 4, \\
x + 2y &= 5.
\end{aligned}
$$

Solve by elimination. Multiply the first by $2$: $4x+2y=8$. Subtract the second
($x+2y=5$): $(4x+2y)-(x+2y)=8-5$, i.e. $3x=3$, so $x=1$. Back-substitute into the first:
$2(1)+y=4\Rightarrow y=2$. The unique stationary point is $(x^\star,y^\star)=(1,2)$.

**Step 3 — the Hessian test.** Differentiate the gradient components again:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2}=2,\quad
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2}=2,\quad
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x\partial y}=1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
H=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}.
$$

Use the $2\times2$ shortcut: $\det H = (2)(2)-(1)^2 = 3>0$ and the top-left entry $a=2>0$,
so $H$ is **positive-definite** — a strict local minimum. (Cross-check with eigenvalues:
they are $2\pm1=\{3,1\}$, both positive, agreeing with @eq-eig.) Since $H$ is constant and
positive-definite *everywhere*, the bowl never bends back down, so $(1,2)$ is the **global**
minimum. The minimum cost is $f(1,2)=1+2+4-4-10+10=3$. @fig-ex1 confirms the hand
arithmetic.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex1
#| fig-cap: "Worked example 1. The coupled cost $f(x,y)=x^2+xy+y^2-4x-5y+10$ as a contour map, with the negative-gradient field. Setting $\\nabla f=\\mathbf{0}$ solves a $2\\times 2$ linear system to the unique stationary point $(1,2)$ (green), confirmed a minimum by the positive-definite Hessian ($\\det H=3>0$). The downhill arrows all flow into it; the tilted elliptical contours are the visual signature of the coupling term $xy$."
f = lambda X, Y: X**2 + X*Y + Y**2 - 4*X - 5*Y + 10
xs = np.linspace(-1, 4, 80)
ys = np.linspace(-1, 5, 80)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(xs, ys)
Z = f(X, Y)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
cs = ax.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=18, colors=GREY, linewidths=0.7, alpha=0.7)
xq = np.linspace(-0.5, 3.5, 14); yq = np.linspace(-0.5, 4.5, 14)
Xq, Yq = np.meshgrid(xq, yq)
U, V = -(2*Xq + Yq - 4), -(Xq + 2*Yq - 5)   # -gradient
ax.quiver(Xq, Yq, U, V, color=TANGENT, alpha=0.7, width=0.004, scale=70)
ax.scatter([1], [2], color=ACCENT, s=90, zorder=6, edgecolor="white",
           label=r"min $(1,2)$, $f=3$")
ax.set_xlabel("staffing $x$"); ax.set_ylabel("inventory $y$")
ax.set_title("Coupled cost: $\\nabla f=\\mathbf{0}$ at one point")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"gradient at (1,2) = ({2*1+2-4}, {1+2*2-5})  (should be (0,0))")
print(f"Hessian det = {2*2 - 1*1}  (>0), top-left = 2 (>0)  => positive-definite => minimum")
print(f"minimum value f(1,2) = {f(1.0,2.0):.1f}")
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"For a general quadratic $f(\mathbf{x})=\tfrac12\mathbf{x}^\top A\mathbf{x} -
\mathbf{b}^\top\mathbf{x} + c$ with $A$ symmetric, where is the minimum?"* Take the
gradient — for a quadratic it is $\nabla f = A\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{b}$ (the multivariate
analogue of $\frac{d}{dx}(\tfrac12 ax^2-bx)=ax-b$) — and set it to zero:
$A\mathbf{x}^\star=\mathbf{b}$, so $\mathbf{x}^\star=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ when $A$ is
invertible. The Hessian is exactly $A$, so it is a minimum **iff $A$ is positive-definite**,
a maximum iff negative-definite, a saddle if indefinite, and if $A$ is singular there may
be no unique solution at all. This is the master template: *every* smooth function looks
like this quadratic up close (its second-order Taylor model), which is precisely what
Newton's method ([OPTM-09]) exploits — it minimizes this local quadratic at every step.
And the OLS normal equations of @sec-ols are this template with $A=X^\top X$ and
$\mathbf{b}=X^\top\mathbf{y}$.
:::

# Worked example 2 — the headline: deriving the OLS normal equations {#sec-ols}

This is the example the Regression track ([REGR-01], [REGR-05]) links here for, and the
reason this article is load-bearing. [REGR-01] derived the one-predictor least-squares line
by minimizing over *two* scalars, the intercept and slope, with two partial derivatives. We
now do the general case — $p$ predictors at once — in one clean stroke, because the gradient
handles all $p+1$ coefficients simultaneously. The payoff is the **normal equations** in
their matrix form, the equation every linear-model solver in the series is built on.

## The setup {#sec-ols-setup}

We have $n$ data points, each with $p$ predictor values and one outcome. Stack them into a
**design matrix** $X$ (size $n\times p$, one row per data point, one column per predictor —
including a column of ones for the intercept) and an **outcome vector** $\mathbf{y}$
(length $n$). A candidate coefficient vector $\boldsymbol\beta\in\mathbb{R}^p$ predicts
$X\boldsymbol\beta$, and the **residual vector** is $\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta$. The
least-squares objective is the **sum of squared residuals**, written compactly as a squared
Euclidean norm:

$$
f(\boldsymbol\beta) = \|\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta\|^2
= (\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta)^\top(\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta),
$$ {#eq-ols-obj}

using the defining fact $\|\mathbf{a}\|^2=\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}$ (a vector's squared
length is its dot product with itself). Our task is exactly the article's theme:
$\arg\min_{\boldsymbol\beta} f(\boldsymbol\beta)$, found by $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$. The
unknown is now the *vector* $\boldsymbol\beta$.

## Expanding the objective {#sec-ols-expand}

Multiply out the product @eq-ols-obj using the rule $(\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b})^\top
(\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b} -
\mathbf{b}^\top\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b}^\top\mathbf{b}$, with
$\mathbf{a}=\mathbf{y}$ and $\mathbf{b}=X\boldsymbol\beta$:

$$
f(\boldsymbol\beta)
= \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y}
- \mathbf{y}^\top X\boldsymbol\beta
- (X\boldsymbol\beta)^\top\mathbf{y}
+ (X\boldsymbol\beta)^\top(X\boldsymbol\beta).
$$

Two simplifications. First, the middle two terms are equal: $(X\boldsymbol\beta)^\top
\mathbf{y} = \boldsymbol\beta^\top X^\top\mathbf{y}$ is a $1\times1$ quantity (a scalar),
and a scalar equals its own transpose, so $\boldsymbol\beta^\top X^\top\mathbf{y} =
(\boldsymbol\beta^\top X^\top\mathbf{y})^\top = \mathbf{y}^\top X\boldsymbol\beta$. Hence
the two cross terms combine into $-2\,\mathbf{y}^\top X\boldsymbol\beta$. Second, the last
term uses $(X\boldsymbol\beta)^\top = \boldsymbol\beta^\top X^\top$, giving
$\boldsymbol\beta^\top X^\top X\boldsymbol\beta$. So

$$
f(\boldsymbol\beta)
= \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y}
- 2\,\mathbf{y}^\top X\boldsymbol\beta
+ \boldsymbol\beta^\top (X^\top X)\,\boldsymbol\beta.
$$ {#eq-ols-expanded}

This is *exactly* the general quadratic of the previous callout, with $A=2X^\top X$,
$\mathbf{b}=2X^\top\mathbf{y}$, and $c=\mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y}$. Reading it confirms our
template: a constant term, a linear term in $\boldsymbol\beta$, and a quadratic term with
matrix $X^\top X$.

## Taking the gradient {#sec-ols-grad}

We differentiate @eq-ols-expanded term by term with respect to the vector $\boldsymbol\beta$.
We need two **matrix-calculus identities**, both of which are the vector versions of facts
you know from Calc 1, and we state each with its 1-D shadow so nothing is opaque:

- **Linear term.** For a constant vector $\mathbf{a}$,
  $\nabla_{\boldsymbol\beta}\bigl(\mathbf{a}^\top\boldsymbol\beta\bigr)=\mathbf{a}$. (Shadow:
  $\frac{d}{d\beta}(a\beta)=a$. The function $\mathbf{a}^\top\boldsymbol\beta=\sum_j
  a_j\beta_j$ has $\partial/\partial\beta_k = a_k$, which stacked is $\mathbf{a}$.) Here the
  linear term is $-2\,\mathbf{y}^\top X\boldsymbol\beta = -2(X^\top\mathbf{y})^\top
  \boldsymbol\beta$, so its gradient is $-2X^\top\mathbf{y}$.
- **Quadratic term.** For a *symmetric* matrix $M$,
  $\nabla_{\boldsymbol\beta}\bigl(\boldsymbol\beta^\top M\boldsymbol\beta\bigr)=2M
  \boldsymbol\beta$. (Shadow: $\frac{d}{d\beta}(m\beta^2)=2m\beta$.) The matrix
  $M=X^\top X$ is symmetric — $(X^\top X)^\top = X^\top (X^\top)^\top = X^\top X$ — so the
  identity applies and the quadratic term's gradient is $2X^\top X\boldsymbol\beta$.

We *derive* the quadratic identity once, so it is not a black box. Write
$\boldsymbol\beta^\top M\boldsymbol\beta=\sum_i\sum_j M_{ij}\beta_i\beta_j$ and
differentiate with respect to $\beta_k$. The terms that involve $\beta_k$ are those with
$i=k$ or $j=k$. Using the product rule, the $k$-th partial collects $\sum_j M_{kj}\beta_j$
(from $i=k$) plus $\sum_i M_{ik}\beta_i$ (from $j=k$); since $M$ is symmetric
($M_{ik}=M_{ki}$) these two sums are equal, giving $2\sum_j M_{kj}\beta_j = 2(M
\boldsymbol\beta)_k$. Stacking over $k$ yields $\nabla = 2M\boldsymbol\beta$, as claimed.

The constant term $\mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y}$ has no $\boldsymbol\beta$, so its gradient is
$\mathbf{0}$. Assembling the three pieces:

$$
\nabla f(\boldsymbol\beta)
= \mathbf{0} \;-\; 2X^\top\mathbf{y} \;+\; 2X^\top X\boldsymbol\beta
= 2\bigl(X^\top X\boldsymbol\beta - X^\top\mathbf{y}\bigr).
$$ {#eq-ols-gradient}

## Setting it to zero: the normal equations {#sec-ols-normal}

Stationarity @thm-stationarity sets @eq-ols-gradient to the zero vector. The constant $2$
divides out (it cannot move where a zero is):

$$
X^\top X\,\hat{\boldsymbol\beta} - X^\top\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{0}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\boxed{\;X^\top X\,\hat{\boldsymbol\beta} = X^\top\mathbf{y}.\;}
$$ {#eq-normal}

These are the **normal equations** — the single matrix equation that generalizes the two
scalar normal equations of [REGR-01]. When $X^\top X$ is invertible (the columns of $X$ are
linearly independent — the multivariate version of "the predictors are not redundant"), the
solution is

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol\beta} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}.
$$ {#eq-ols-solution}

We can now state the result formally.

::: {#thm-ols}
**Theorem (OLS coefficients).** If $X^\top X$ is positive-definite (equivalently, the
columns of the $n\times p$ design matrix $X$ are linearly independent, which requires
$n\ge p$), then $f(\boldsymbol\beta)=\|\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta\|^2$ has the unique
minimizer $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}=(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top\mathbf{y}$, characterized by the
normal equations $X^\top X\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}=X^\top\mathbf{y}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Existence and the formula are the derivation @sec-ols-setup–@sec-ols-normal: $\nabla
f=\mathbf{0}$ (@eq-ols-gradient set to zero) gives the normal equations @eq-normal, whose
solution is @eq-ols-solution. That this stationary point is a *minimum* — not a maximum or
saddle — follows from the Hessian test @thm-hessian: differentiating @eq-ols-gradient again
gives the constant Hessian $H = 2X^\top X$. The matrix $X^\top X$ is positive-definite by
hypothesis, so $H$ is too (all eigenvalues positive by @eq-eig), confirming a strict
minimum; and since $H$ is positive-definite *everywhere*, it is the global minimum.
Uniqueness follows because $X^\top X$ positive-definite is invertible, so @eq-normal has
exactly one solution. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Notice that $X^\top X$ playing the role of "the curvature" is the multivariate echo of
[REGR-01]'s $S_{xx}>0$ condition: there, the single number $S_{xx}$ had to be positive for a
unique line; here, the *matrix* $X^\top X$ must be positive-definite for a unique fit. The
degenerate boundary is the same idea generalized — if a predictor is a copy (or linear
combination) of others, $X^\top X$ is singular, the inverse @eq-ols-solution does not exist,
and there is no unique best fit. That is **multicollinearity**, the multivariate face of
[REGR-01]'s "no spread in $x$" failure, treated fully in [REGR-07]. @fig-ols runs the
derivation on real data and verifies $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ numerically.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ols
#| fig-cap: "The headline example on real data. We fit a two-predictor least-squares model (so $\\boldsymbol\\beta\\in\\mathbb{R}^3$ with an intercept) by solving the normal equations $X^\\top X\\hat{\\boldsymbol\\beta}=X^\\top\\mathbf{y}$, i.e. $\\nabla\\|\\mathbf{y}-X\\boldsymbol\\beta\\|^2=\\mathbf{0}$. **Left:** observed vs. fitted outcomes hug the 45-degree line, the model fits. **Right:** the residuals against fitted values scatter flat around zero — and their gradient-defining property holds, $X^\\top(\\mathbf{y}-X\\hat{\\boldsymbol\\beta})=\\mathbf{0}$, printed below to machine precision. The closed form is exactly the stationary point of this article."
rng = np.random.default_rng(2024)
n = 80
x1 = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)           # e.g. advertising spend
x2 = rng.uniform(0, 5, n)            # e.g. store footfall (thousands)
# true relationship with real noise
y = 3.0 + 1.4*x1 - 0.8*x2 + rng.normal(0, 2.0, n)

X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), x1, x2])   # design matrix with intercept column
# solve the normal equations (use solve, not explicit inverse, for stability)
beta_hat = np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X, X.T @ y)
fitted = X @ beta_hat
resid = y - fitted

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.7))
lim = [min(y.min(), fitted.min()), max(y.max(), fitted.max())]
axL.plot(lim, lim, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2)
axL.scatter(fitted, y, color=CURVE, s=28, alpha=0.85)
axL.set_xlabel("fitted $\\hat y$"); axL.set_ylabel("observed $y$")
axL.set_title("Observed vs. fitted")

axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axR.scatter(fitted, resid, color=CURVE, s=28, alpha=0.85)
axR.set_xlabel("fitted $\\hat y$"); axR.set_ylabel("residual")
axR.set_title("Residuals (flat = good)")
plt.tight_layout()

grad = 2*(X.T @ X @ beta_hat - X.T @ y)
print(f"beta_hat (intercept, x1, x2) = {np.round(beta_hat, 3)}")
print(f"gradient at beta_hat         = {np.round(grad, 8)}   (== 0, stationarity)")
print(f"X^T (y - X beta) [normal eqns] = {np.round(X.T @ resid, 8)}   (== 0)")
print(f"X^T X positive-definite? eigenvalues = {np.round(np.linalg.eigvalsh(X.T @ X), 2)}  (all > 0)")
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why are they called the 'normal' equations, and what is the geometry?"* "Normal" means
**perpendicular**, not "ordinary." The normal equations @eq-normal rearrange to
$X^\top(\mathbf{y}-X\hat{\boldsymbol\beta})=\mathbf{0}$ — the residual vector
$\mathbf{y}-X\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}$ is **orthogonal to every column of $X$** (each row of
the equation is one column of $X$ dotted with the residual, set to zero). Geometrically,
$\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}$ projects $\mathbf{y}$ onto the subspace spanned by the predictors,
and the residual is what is left over — perpendicular to that subspace, the shortest
possible leftover. That projection view is owned by [REGR-04 *Regression as Projection*],
which derives the *same* $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}$ from geometry instead of calculus. Here
the takeaway is that "set the gradient to zero" and "make the residual perpendicular to the
predictors" are the identical statement — the gradient @eq-ols-gradient *is*
$-2X^\top(\text{residual})$, so it vanishes exactly when the residual is orthogonal to the
columns.
:::

# Failure modes and the honest boundary {#sec-failure}

We have met the new failure mode (the saddle); collect all the ways "$\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$"
can mislead, because knowing the failure surface separates "can take a gradient" from "knows
when to trust one." Each mirrors a 1-D failure from [OPTM-01], with a multivariate twist.

## Stationary but not a minimum: the saddle, in the wild {#sec-saddle-wild}

The pure saddle $x^2-y^2$ was a teaching example; saddles also lurk inside realistic
objectives. Consider $f(x,y) = x^4 - 2x^2 + y^2$ — a "double-well in $x$, single-well in
$y$" shape that appears in physics and in loss surfaces. Its gradient is

$$
\nabla f = \begin{pmatrix} 4x^3 - 4x \\ 2y \end{pmatrix},
$$

which vanishes when $y=0$ and $4x(x^2-1)=0$, i.e. at $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, and $(-1,0)$ —
**three** stationary points. The Hessian is $H=\begin{psmallmatrix}12x^2-4 & 0\\0 & 2
\end{psmallmatrix}$. At $(\pm1,0)$: $H=\begin{psmallmatrix}8&0\\0&2\end{psmallmatrix}$,
positive-definite — two genuine minima. At $(0,0)$: $H=\begin{psmallmatrix}-4&0\\0&2
\end{psmallmatrix}$, eigenvalues $-4$ and $2$, **indefinite** — a saddle. Reporting $(0,0)$
as "the minimum" because the gradient vanishes there is exactly the error the Hessian test
exists to catch. @fig-saddle-wild shows it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-saddle-wild
#| fig-cap: "A saddle hiding among real minima. The objective $f(x,y)=x^4-2x^2+y^2$ has three stationary points (all with $\\nabla f=\\mathbf{0}$): two true minima at $(\\pm1,0)$ where the Hessian is positive-definite (green), and a **saddle** at the origin where it is indefinite (red X) — down along $x$ toward either well, up along $y$. Solving $\\nabla f=\\mathbf{0}$ alone cannot tell these apart; the Hessian eigenvalues do."
f = lambda X, Y: X**4 - 2*X**2 + Y**2
xs = np.linspace(-1.7, 1.7, 90); ys = np.linspace(-1.4, 1.4, 90)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(xs, ys)
Z = f(X, Y)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=np.linspace(-1.0, 3.0, 22), colors=GREY, linewidths=0.7, alpha=0.7)
ax.scatter([1, -1], [0, 0], color=ACCENT, s=90, zorder=6, edgecolor="white",
           label=r"minima $(\pm1,0)$: pos-def")
ax.scatter([0], [0], color=TANGENT, marker="X", s=120, zorder=6,
           label=r"saddle $(0,0)$: indefinite")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
ax.set_title(r"Three stationary points, one is a saddle")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
for px, py in [(0,0), (1,0), (-1,0)]:
    H = np.array([[12*px**2 - 4, 0],[0, 2.0]])
    eig = np.linalg.eigvalsh(H)
    kind = "minimum" if (eig>0).all() else ("maximum" if (eig<0).all() else "SADDLE")
    print(f"({px:+d},{py}): Hessian eigenvalues {eig}  ->  {kind}")
```

## The inconclusive case: a semidefinite Hessian {#sec-semidefinite}

When the Hessian has a zero eigenvalue it is only **semidefinite**, and @thm-hessian goes
silent — the exact multivariate echo of $f''=0$ in [OPTM-01]. The quadratic term is flat
along the zero-eigenvalue direction, so the *cubic and higher* terms decide, and the test
cannot see them. The cleanest witnesses, paralleling [OPTM-01]'s $x^4$/$-x^4$/$x^3$ trio,
share the Hessian $H=\begin{psmallmatrix}2&0\\0&0\end{psmallmatrix}$ (positive-semidefinite,
eigenvalues $2$ and $0$) at the origin yet have three different truths:

- $f=x^2+y^4$: a **minimum** (flat-but-rising along $y$, like $y^4$);
- $f=x^2-y^4$: a **saddle** (falls along $y$, like $-y^4$);
- $f=x^2+y^3$: **neither** (the $y^3$ term is an inflection along $y$).

All three have the *same* gradient $\nabla f=(2x,\,*)^\top$ vanishing at the origin and the
*same* semidefinite Hessian, so the Hessian test returns "inconclusive" for every one. The
honest resolution mirrors OPTM-01's higher-order test: examine $f$ along the flat direction
directly. @fig-semidefinite shows the three slices.

```{python}
#| label: fig-semidefinite
#| fig-cap: "The inconclusive case. All three objectives have the same positive-semidefinite Hessian at the origin (eigenvalues $2$ and $0$), so the Hessian test is silent on every one. Sliced along the flat $y$-direction ($x=0$): $x^2+y^4$ rises (a minimum), $x^2-y^4$ falls (a saddle), and $x^2+y^3$ does neither (inflection). A zero eigenvalue is the test going silent, not a verdict -- the multivariate echo of OPTM-01's $f''=0$ case."
ys = np.linspace(-1.2, 1.2, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(ys, ys**4,  color=ACCENT,  label=r"$y^4$ slice of $x^2+y^4$ (min)")
ax.plot(ys, -ys**4, color=TANGENT, label=r"$-y^4$ slice of $x^2-y^4$ (saddle)")
ax.plot(ys, ys**3,  color=CURVE,   label=r"$y^3$ slice of $x^2+y^3$ (neither)")
ax.scatter([0,0,0],[0,0,0], color="black", s=30, zorder=5)
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel("$y$ (the flat Hessian direction, at $x=0$)"); ax.set_ylabel("$f(0,y)$")
ax.set_title("Same semidefinite Hessian, three different truths")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_ylim(-1.3, 1.6)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Many valleys: local versus global {#sec-local-global}

@sec-saddle-wild already showed an objective with *two* minima. The gradient test — and the
Hessian test — are **local**: they certify that a point has no downhill direction nearby,
but cannot tell whether a *deeper* basin exists elsewhere. In $f=x^4-2x^2+y^2$ the two wells
$(\pm1,0)$ happen to be tied, but perturb the function and one becomes strictly lower; a
local search started in the wrong basin converges to the wrong valley with full confidence.
Ruling this out is exactly what **convexity** buys — for a convex function every stationary
point *is* the global minimum — and it is why [OPTM-04] is the dividing line of the field.
In the non-convex landscapes of deep learning, this gap is unavoidable and we settle for a
*good* local minimum.

## Non-differentiable points {#sec-nondiff}

@thm-stationarity assumed differentiability, just as [OPTM-01] did. A function with a
**kink** — like $f(\boldsymbol\beta)=\|\mathbf{y}-X\boldsymbol\beta\|^2 +
\lambda\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_1$, the **lasso** objective, whose $\ell_1$ penalty has corners
wherever a coefficient hits zero — has no gradient at those corners, so $\nabla
f=\mathbf{0}$ cannot even be stated there. The repair is the **subgradient** (the set of all
slopes that fit under the graph at a kink), owned by [OPTM-05], and it is precisely what
makes the lasso solvable. Here we simply flag the boundary: smooth $\Rightarrow$ use the
gradient test; kinked $\Rightarrow$ you need subgradients.

## Constrained minima: the gradient need not vanish {#sec-constrained}

Finally, the multivariate echo of OPTM-01's boundary case. @thm-stationarity required an
**interior** point. If the minimizer sits on the boundary of a constraint set — say
$\boldsymbol\beta$ is forced to satisfy $g(\boldsymbol\beta)=0$ or $\|\boldsymbol\beta\|\le
t$ (the constrained form of ridge/lasso) — the gradient at the optimum can be *nonzero*,
pointing out of the feasible region while the constraint holds you in. "Set the gradient to
zero" is then the wrong condition entirely. The correct generalization is that $\nabla f$
must be *balanced by* the constraint gradients — the **Lagrange multiplier** condition
([OPTM-13]) and, for inequalities, the **KKT conditions** ([OPTM-14]). When you next see a
multiplier, remember it is measuring exactly the nonzero gradient that the wall is pushing
back against.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A zero gradient is necessary, never sufficient, and only for interior, smooth, single-basin
problems.** Four ways a naive "$\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$, done" goes wrong, each with its fix:
**(1)** the point is a **saddle** or maximum, not a minimum — *check the Hessian's
definiteness*; **(2)** the Hessian is **semidefinite** — *look along the flat direction*;
**(3)** there are **multiple basins** and you found a non-global one — *convexity* ([OPTM-04])
or multi-start; **(4)** the minimum is on a **constraint boundary** or at a **kink** — *KKT*
([OPTM-14]) or *subgradients* ([OPTM-05]). The single biggest of these in modern ML is the
saddle: in high dimensions it is overwhelmingly the most common stationary point, and it has
no one-dimensional warning sign.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is 'set the gradient to zero and solve' the right tool, and when is it the wrong
one?"* The honest summary you should be able to recite:

- **Use it** when (a) the objective is **smooth** (differentiable everywhere), (b) you are
  optimizing over an **open/unconstrained** region so the optimum is interior, and (c) the
  resulting system $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ is **solvable** — ideally *linear*, which happens
  exactly when $f$ is quadratic (least squares, ridge, Gaussian MLE). In that regime it gives
  an exact closed-form answer and the Hessian certifies it.
- **Do not use it** when the objective is **non-smooth** (lasso, hinge loss — use
  subgradients/proximal methods, [OPTM-05], [OPTM-16]), when there are **constraints** (use
  Lagrange/KKT, [OPTM-13]–[OPTM-14]), when $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ has **no closed form**
  (logistic regression, neural nets — *iterate* with gradient descent or Newton, [OPTM-06],
  [OPTM-09]), or when the landscape is **non-convex with many basins** (you get a local
  guarantee only).
- **And the deepest caveat:** even a perfectly solved $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ with a
  positive-definite Hessian certifies only a **local** minimum unless you also know the
  function is **convex** ([OPTM-04]).
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You would rarely form
$(X^\top X)^{-1}$ explicitly. For the closed-form quadratic cases (least squares, ridge), a
numerical stack solves the normal equations with a stable factorization —
`numpy.linalg.lstsq(X, y)` or `scipy.linalg.lstsq`, which use a **QR** or **SVD**
decomposition rather than forming and inverting $X^\top X$ (squaring the data into $X^\top X$
loses numerical precision, especially when predictors are nearly collinear). For
*non-quadratic* objectives where $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ has no closed form,
`scipy.optimize.minimize` runs an iterative method — by default **BFGS** (a quasi-Newton
method, [OPTM-09]), which builds a Hessian approximation from gradients — and you supply a
function returning $f$ and, ideally, $\nabla f$; if you only give $f$, it estimates the
gradient by finite differences (slower and noisier). The practitioner's habit worth
internalizing: **check whether $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ is linear first** (it is for every
quadratic loss in this series) and solve it directly; reach for an iterative optimizer only
when the system is nonlinear, and feed it the analytic gradient whenever you can. And after
*any* optimizer returns, the diagnostic of this article still applies — inspect the gradient
norm (is it really near zero?) and, when it matters, the Hessian's eigenvalues (is it
actually a minimum?).

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[OPTM-01 *One-Dimensional Minimization*]**, which solved the
single-variable problem with $f'(x)=0$ and the second-derivative test. This article
generalized both: the derivative became the **gradient** $\nabla f$, the condition $f'=0$
became **stationarity** $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ (derived from the directional-derivative
picture), and the second-derivative test became the **Hessian test** — with the genuinely
new **saddle point** that one dimension cannot show. We cashed it out on the OLS normal
equations, the move the Regression track links here for. The track now forks into two
parallel spines, both starting from this article:

- **The convexity spine, [OPTM-03]–[OPTM-05]**, answers the question this article kept
  deferring: *when is a stationary point the **global** minimum?* [OPTM-04 *Convex
  Functions*] proves that for a convex function, every stationary point is global — turning
  "we found a valley" into "we found *the* valley" — and ties convexity to the Hessian being
  positive-semidefinite *everywhere*. [OPTM-05 *Subgradients*] repairs the non-differentiable
  case (@sec-nondiff).
- **The descent spine, [OPTM-06] onward**, answers the other deferred question: *what do you
  do when $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ has no closed form?* [OPTM-06 *Gradient Descent*] turns
  $-\nabla f$ — the steepest-descent direction we *derived* in @sec-steepest — into the
  iterative algorithm that trains nearly every model in the series. [OPTM-09 *Newton's
  Method*] uses the **Hessian** of this article to take curvature-aware steps.
- **[OPTM-11 *Stochastic Gradient Descent*]** is where the **saddle points** of
  @sec-three-faces become a central practical concern: in high-dimensional ML they are
  everywhere, and the noise in SGD is part of how training escapes them.
- **[OPTM-13 *Lagrange Multipliers*]** and **[OPTM-14 *KKT*]** handle the **constrained** case
  of @sec-constrained, where the gradient at the optimum is nonzero and balanced by the
  constraints.

Downstream, **[REGR-05 *Multiple Regression*]** consumes the normal equations @eq-normal
directly, and **[REGR-04 *Regression as Projection*]** re-derives the same
$\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}$ as a geometric projection — the orthogonality we read off the
gradient in @sec-ols.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The classical statements.** The first-order condition $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ and the
  second-order (Hessian/definiteness) test are standard multivariable-calculus material; any
  rigorous text develops them with the second-order Taylor theorem we used in @eq-taylor2 —
  e.g. Marsden & Tromba, *Vector Calculus*, or the optimization-flavored treatment in
  Nocedal & Wright, *Numerical Optimization*, ch. 2, which states the necessary and
  sufficient conditions exactly as @thm-stationarity and @thm-hessian.
- **Quadratic forms and definiteness** — the linear-algebra engine behind the Hessian test —
  are owned by the Geometry of Data track; the spectral theorem (symmetric matrices have real
  eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors) is the result that makes @eq-eig's
  eigenvalue criterion legitimate. Strang's *Introduction to Linear Algebra* and
  Horn & Johnson's *Matrix Analysis* develop it.
- **Saddle points in high-dimensional optimization.** The claim that saddles, not local
  minima, dominate non-convex ML landscapes is made precise in Dauphin et al., *"Identifying
  and attacking the saddle point problem in high-dimensional non-convex optimization"* (2014),
  and connects to random-matrix theory — the counting argument of @sec-three-faces made
  rigorous.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *When is a stationary point the
  global minimum?* — convexity, [OPTM-04]. *What if $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ has no closed
  form?* — gradient descent and Newton, [OPTM-06], [OPTM-09]. *What if the objective has
  kinks?* — subgradients, [OPTM-05]. *What if the minimum is constrained?* — Lagrange and
  KKT, [OPTM-13]–[OPTM-14]. Each is the harder or more general descendant of a failure mode
  we made explicit in @sec-failure.
